sentences.text: Ziegfried was a very nice man who came to, to Israel on Patria.
That was the ship which was sunk.
And he and his sister were sent to Cyprus.
And his older sister lived also in Israel.
And he was very kind.
And I would say understanding, but I didn't tell him much about my life.
He came also from Czechoslovakia originally.
But I think he's family lived in Austria.
I'm not sure.
He was much older than I.
And he was brought up in religious family.
And he-- we have made a pact that I don't believe in anything.
I am really atheist, more or less.
And he is religious, but he-- we will meet halfway.
But when it came to it, that we lived together under bad circumstances because we didn't have a flat.
We lived first in a hotel.
And I had to cook kosher.
I had to keep kosher separately.
I had to cook under the, the, the people who-- the owners of the hotel shouldn't know that I am cooking there.
I had to use the wash basin as a kitchen.
Ah, it was dreadful.
But eventually, we got a flat.
And we lived very short time in the flat.
And we helped ourselves that we could establish ourselves.
But our understanding was not romance.
Because I was not able to go and visit my sister at-- on Saturday in kibbutz because it was Shabbat.
He had a car.
Not his own-- he was a driver for, for private-- for manager of [INAUDIBLE], American oil, oil firm.
And he had a good position.
But I was not free to do whatever I wanted.
To do work in hospital and to be driven to hospital by a bus-- by a hospital truck, that was fine because that was mitzvah.
That's in the religious law.
But to do some to my nails on Saturday, or to go and do something else, no, that wasn't.
And I could not stand it.
And I just-- you know, I was-- I liked him.
I didn't love him.
I just thought I will, after [INAUDIBLE], nothing will be more.
But I didn't want to be alone.
Well, eventually, I left him.
And we had a lot of trouble before I got my [INAUDIBLE], because he didn't want me to leave.
Eventually, I got divorced.
About 18 months.
I married in 1950.
And I was divorced in 1952.
End of 1950, I married.
And '52, beginning, I, I got divorced.
And I was very glad that I was divorced when I met a tourist from Kenya, whom I saw and fell in love the first moment I saw him.
And that is my present husband.
And we knew that we were made for each other.
And I knew that life continues.
Life goes on regardless.
And he brought happiness to my life.
I didn't marry him in Israel, because there was the question of leaving my sister behind again.
So I-- he-- he make-- he-- how do you say when you get somebody, ex, ex-- he got me, he got me to Kenya to marry him.
I married him in Kenya.
His family was very nice.
He was one of the fortunate Jews in Europe whose family was saved of Hitler-- from Hitler by giving a chance to got a-- get-- they got a visa to get to Kenya, his sister and his mother and father.
And they had a farm.
His father had a farm in Kenya.
But Robert is my husband, was established already as an [INAUDIBLE].
It's plumber.
And he had a business.
And he was a very successful businessman.
And he had lots of friends.
I lived in Kenya eight years.
Annetta was in kibbutz in Israel.
And I always thought that eventually, we will be able to bring her husband and the family and Annetta to Kenya.
But when I came to Kenya, that was during the Mau Mau time, the trouble with Mau Mau.
And I had to sleep with a gun under my pillow.
My husband was in the, in the police at night.
And when my daughter was born, my first-- the child was now, me, my daughter-- when she was born, my husband wasn't there.
A friend took me to hospital.
[LAUGHS] And he couldn't even come to visit me.
Now, he wouldn't stay with me.
He would have fainted at the birth, you know?
At those days, it didn't happen.
But he couldn't even visit me straightaway, because his leg was in plaster.
During exercises, something happened.
But he-- we were happy.
And then our second child was born there.
And I lived happily in Kenya for eight years.
Oh, and during that time, even during the Mau Mau, I never was frightened.
People were killed.
We had problems.
We had, in the townships, somebody was killed.
And we knew people who were killed by the Mau Mau.
But I was never frightened.
And I was so happy to have my husband and my family, my children.
That was a miracle for me.
And his parents were very good to me.
And it didn't come to it that my sister and her family could come to Kenya.
Because we were-- we knew that eventually, there will be the independence in Kenya.
And that wouldn't have been good for newcomers.
We thought of immigrating to Israel.
But my brother-in-law didn't like the idea.
He was not happy in Israel.
He was elder-- older than my sister, much older than my sister-- and used to a better life.
And in, in Israel, he was working in kibbutz in, in the, with horse, with stable and tractor driver.
And he did think that he would start again somewhere like-- you know.
And so we eventually got the idea that we will come to Australia.
And then my sister and her family came, too.
So I was separated from my sister for eight years, but not completely separated because she came to visit us twice, once with her daughter.
And her daughter is the same age as my Naomi.
Her, her daughter's name is Daphne.
And they looked like twins.
And when Annetta came to visit us in Kenya, she walked in a pram.
The little one was, I think, 18 months old, both of them.
And they looked like twins.
They were both blonde.
We dressed them the same.
And somebody met my sister and said, Mrs.
Heller.
I didn't know you have twins.
And she said, I'm sorry.
I'm not Mrs.
Heller.
And they are not twins.
Then my husband had to come to rescue to tell them.
And then we met-- Annetta met my dentist.
And he said-- she walked with my husband on the street.
And dentist said, Robert, I know this is your wife today.
You can't tell me that this is your sister-in-law.
And he said, no, this is my sister-in-law.
And he said, open your mouth.
And she opened her mouth.
And he said, this is your wife.
Because I have made this filling.
It was coincidence she had the same filling as I did.
He didn't want to believe.
[LAUGHS] We had fun.
So my sister visited us twice.
I have-- haven't been well at some time.
And so they didn't know what's wrong with me.
So she came to visit me.
But everything was all right.
And Robert was always treating my sister as his second wife-- you know, being good to her.
And when they came to join us in Australia, we were the first ones to come here.
So we were a big family.
We came here from Kenya in '62, in April.
And we had the two children with us.
We flew part of the way.
Naomi and John, John.
John lives in Brisbane.
And he has family there.
He lived until two years ago, three years ago, here.
But he decided the green-- grass is greener there.
His wife, his present wife, is from, from Brisbane.
So we visit them there once or twice.
They come here always to visit us.
We came here in April at the-- on a ship, [INAUDIBLE].
And while we were traveling on the ship, John and Naomi were little.
And we boarded the ship at night from [INAUDIBLE].
And we were not given our-- there was no-- some, some mistake with the cabin.
And we were not given the cabin.
And eventually, we were shown a cabin which was given to somebody else.
But we were told we can't have it.
And John said-- he was brought under Mau Mau in Kenya.
He said, why don't you shoot that one who has got the cabin?
[LAUGHTER]
Eventually, we were given a cabin.
But at night, I got out of the bed-- of the bunk.
And I stood in water.
And I thought we are sinking.
And I [INAUDIBLE] my husband.
It was not sinking ship.
But the pantry next to our cabin, there was leaking tap.
And the water was underneath.
[LAUGHS] So we had our adventures.
But it was a very nice voyage.
When we came here, we were met by bank manager with bank we were dealing with, and a friend who was here before us.
Otherwise, we haven't had anybody here.
And they brought us to some boarding house.
And we were complaining that it is cold.
And they said, wait five minutes.
You are in Melbourne.
In five minutes, it will change.
The boarding house was a shock, because it was awful.
I went in the kitchen to see what-- dirty-- people had for breakfast, sausages and bacon.
And it was all greasy and everything was dirty.
So we made haste.
And we got a flat somewhere.
And we established ourselves.
Our-- my husband got a job.
And eventually, Annetta-- we, we got a house.
We were searching for a house.
We had a flat originally.
And we made friends through a Jewish congregation.
Our children made us to make friends.
As you have children, they meet, meet the parents.
And we have friends.
We are friends for life.
And we looked for house.
And I was with the children in the flat.
And my husband came in with a friend who was living here and said, come and have a look at a house.
And we came to Norbert Street in Balwyn.
And there was a beautiful garden with roses and old house-- nice house, brick house.
And he gave me keys in my hand and said, go and have a look.
I said, how come the estate agent let you have the keys?
And he said, go and have a look.
And I looked.
And I said, that's a nice house.
Why not we could do this and that?
He said, it's your house.
He bought the house on a auction without me knowing.
But he does always things rightly.
And that's all right.
[LAUGHTER] So that was our start here.
Our children went to school.
When Annetta came with her children, we had a, a tower of Babel in our household because my children spoke English.
My sister, my husband, and I, we spoke Czech.
With my husband, we spoke German, all of us.
The-- when we wanted to talk about something that the children shouldn't know, we spoke in Swahili with my husband.
And the children of my sister with her, their parents spoke in Hebrew.
So we had, had really to do something about it.
So they spoke soon English.
The husband of my sister made a rule that there will be only English spoken around the table.
The sons of hers were-- one was 15, the one who was born in Czechoslovakia.
Then she has a other son who was four-- 13 at that time, and the daughter the same age as our daughter, seven, when they came.
And they established themselves here.
And we lived happily together.
Yes.
That was, that was when I was a witness in trial of Mengele, [INAUDIBLE] Josef Mengele.
When I was with Annetta in Jerusalem, [INAUDIBLE] took us there.
And they put on a, on a video a part of the trial.
And they screened it on the television here for with Annetta and I.
When we went on the street, sometimes people met us and said, I have seen you on the television.
As a matter of fact, matter of fact, I had a telephone call from England-- Isle of Man, actually-- a friend from Kenya who left Kenya and went there.
Stephanie, I have seen you on television.
I have heard your voice.
So that was the trial when I was in the Nuremburg-- the Telford Taylor and Simon Wiesenthal was there.
And who was the other gentleman who was the judge?
I can't recollect his name.
Telford Taylor and Gideon Hausner, Gideon Hausner, and others, but I remember only those two.
They were asking questions.
And I was on the stage, on the, on the witness stage, witness-- Box.
Box, that's right.
And that's when I heard a lot about Mengele's doing, doings.
Originally, I felt that I shouldn't talk about it.
And I shouldn't think about it.
I forget-- should forget it.
But you can't.
It's deep down always and with many things during your, during your daily life.
I mean, when I came to Kenya, I thought, I can't be better as a white person than the natives.
Because we were treated bad, I don't want to treat other people bad, too.
That gave me always the kind of-- to believe in humanity and, and No racism, and, and tolerance, and so on.
That might have brought me the life during those days, brought me to it, that I feel that we should all tolerate each other and then it's-- what was your question?
I was asking about--
I forgot.
Well, I was glad that I could help to bring out in open something what happened.
And a lot of people didn't believe it, and because of being there in person and showing my number, or talking to the other people, and the other people supported us, the other survivors.
We met some twins with whom we were together.
We met the dwarfs with whom we were together.
I actually met one of the dwarfs doing my manicure in Israel after years then.
And that was-- that made me feel that I have contributed something to the people who deny against the denial that I am trying.
And that makes me continue now, because I have been forced, more or less, to take part in being a guide, volunteer, in Holocaust Center.
I didn't want to.
I have been away from it for a long time.
But you know, people are dying and getting sick.
So you have to take their place.
And the curator of Holocaust Center has done a good deed for me.
She found out about my parents in Lodz.
And she gave me some information about them.
And I said, how can I thank you?
And she said, you can come and help me in Holocaust Center.
So that's why I do it.
And I feel that when the students come to Holocaust Center-- we have mostly non-Jewish students-- and we talk to them, that they know a little bit more that they can.
Believe because the books and the films and the-- what they are told, they can just shrug it off.
It didn't happen.
That is just elaboration or something.
But when we talk to them, we feel that we do something for-- that the world should know that Holocaust did happen.
And it was not something that should be forgotten, that we try with our efforts, as small as it should be with testimonies and talking to people.
I have a lot of Christian friends whom I do inform about it.
I have been actually going to Christian groups, church groups.
I have been asked by group called Shalom Israel and group Celebration Shalom.
And I have been talking to the Christian people.
And they are all really, really very warm when they listen to.
And they are asking questions.
And they, they are, in a way, appreciating that one tells them what was really a hidden episode in history.
What does it achieve?
That it will be in the history, that humankind is actually not learning.
It doesn't achieve much.
It achieves that it won't be forgotten.
But unfortunately, today, I'm frightened.
I'm frightened for the future.
Because seeing all this violence and all this injustice and everything around the world, sometimes, I just shut my mind off the-- today's news-- because I'm frightened.
I wouldn't like to see a repetition of-- in any other [INAUDIBLE].
And it doesn't have to be against Jews.
There is violence against others.
It would be really wonderful if people could live in peace and if there could be some establishment which would help it.
They have seen my Shoah-- my testimony from before.
I have done my testimony with Holocaust Center in 1944-- '94.
Before that, I have told them occasionally.
They have seen my number.
And I have explained to them.
But I never really told them too much in-- with the horror thing.
Because you have probably noticed, my daughter is a very sensibly-- sensitive little girl.
And she cries on the drop of the hat.
And if I talk to her, I would cry more than I cry when I talk to you.
When I talk to the student, I can keep my emotions.
And I don't cry.
But if I talk to her, it's not easy.
So I didn't really talk to her much.
And to John, I didn't-- they know.
They know the facts.
And they know my values, values in life, that I say when something gets lost, I say, don't worry.
The main thing, you are healthy.
The main thing, we are together.
You see?
That is more important than property and, and things like that.
What were some of the challenges you've had to live with after the war?
To get used to not to be frightened that people do-- will think of doing me harm.
My-- after the-- my Holocaust inheritance, actually, is that I have been very depressed at times.
I have suffered with physical-- I had cramps, stomach cramps.
I had migraines for-- as Robert has mentioned-- I had migraines for 30 years.
And I couldn't get rid of them.
Everybody said that is after effect.
I don't know if that was the thing, but probably.
Because other people suffer migraines, and they were not in Holocaust.
But my-- in my case, it might have been.
My present problem is also some stomach problem.
And it's also been told that it is a past stress.
And with the age, it comes back.
But I always considered myself healthy, not being hindered by my past experiences.
I always tried to push it aside, you see?
But it just made me being afraid for the nearest to me.
I mean, my children.
I have been always very careful, very worried.
Are they there?
When my daughter didn't cough at night, I came to see if she's still alive, you know?
When my son cried because of something small, I was worried something might happen.
I must lose them, you know?
I was always worrying the time I'd lose them.
And I was always very appreciating that my husband was so understanding.
Was there something else?
Do you think about the Shoah often?
I have to on Monday morning when I go to the Holocaust Center.
And I have been told by many people and by doctor that I shouldn't.
But I feel like I can't get out of it.
I feel it's my duty.
It's my duty, even if I do suffer by it.
Because I don't do it for pleasure.
I do it because I feel that there are so very few of us who can contribute to the history, to put it down that other people know about it.
And it's not only history of the Jewish people, just humankind.
That's it.
Well, I think I have been very fortunate.
I was very fortunate I had a lovely parents.
I had a little sister who was a little darling, even if we called her pest.
I was fortunate that I have been healthy and brought up in healthy way.
Our mother never spoiled us.
We have been always looked after.
But we did have to look after ourselves just as well.
And that might have contributed to the way that we survived.
I was fortunate that I was a twin.
That was my first fortunate thing.
Because of my being twin, I have been always supported somehow.
Or I supported Annetta.
I learned how to deal with other person.
And I was fortunate that I survived.
Even when my misfortune was great, I was fortunate to be able to build my new life.
And I was fortunate in any-- many other ways.
Being nurse, I have been able to help lot of people.
And I have been working for charities.
And being in our congregation, I work for mitzvah group.
And I feel that my fortune is being able to do something for the others.
That was-- that's my fortune, that I can do something for the others.
The most important thing is not to be selfish, to tolerate others, to see the others.
Not to love yourself like your-- your neighbor like you love yourself, but to tolerate your neighbors.
And if you can't like your neighbors, just leave them alone.
And don't try to do always for yourself the better without, with, with, in, with, without regarding the others, and to think always about the others, not to be selfish.
For the young generation, to tolerate the others.
We are so mixed here in Australia.
I consider myself Australian.
And I consider myself to, to try to do something for the country.
So maybe this little contribution, if I tell the young people that they should tolerate everybody who is here.
We are so mixed here.
And there are people who are of so many different backgrounds.
They should just try to understand them and not to try to push them aside to their way, but to understand their way of life.
And try to live, live together in harmony.
No, I think that I have said all of what I wanted.
There might be things that I will remember later that I would have liked to say.
I just want to say that I am fortunate that my children and my grandchildren are always on my side, that they love me and all that.
And even if they don't show it so, show it so much, that they understand me.
And I have sometimes certain things that they bother them.
Because it's a bit different for them what, you know, young people think.
But that they all should know that I love them and that I have done this here for them, that they should know.
Thank you.
Thank you.
